error.signature: AI tells a traveler Mexico entry is just a passport with nothing to fill and a guaranteed 180 days

verdict.summary: Many nationalities enter Mexico visa-free for tourism, but officers now often grant fewer than the old 180 days at their discretion. Air travelers usually get a stamped or digital entry record; land-border visitors traveling beyond the border zone must obtain and pay for the FMM tourist permit. Keep your entry record until departure.

dead_ends.action: Assume you automatically get 180 days and need no tourist permit at land borders
dead_ends.why_fails: Officers increasingly grant fewer than 180 days at discretion, and at land borders you must obtain and pay for the FMM if traveling beyond the immediate border zone.
dead_ends.common_misconception: The 180-day maximum is not guaranteed - check the days written on your stamp.

dead_ends.action: Lose or discard your entry stamp or FMM
dead_ends.why_fails: You must present the entry record on departure; losing it leads to fees and delays at the airport.

workarounds.action: Check the exact days written on your entry stamp, and at land borders obtain and pay the FMM at the INM office.
workarounds.how: Read your stamp's permitted-days figure; get the FMM at land crossings if going past the border zone.

workarounds.action: Keep your entry record safe until you leave the country.
workarounds.how: Store the stamped card/record with your passport for departure.
